turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: They filed.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: They filed within the 30-day rule --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So that -- so that there was a compliance with the state law where some nugatory by the fact that he was an escapee, is that it?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. Counsel did everything that was physically possible for them to do.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, that was the different situation from Brown and Allen. There was a meeting that -- that the State requirement was met.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: It was --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But -- but the Indiana Supreme Court says, “It isn't met because we don't entertain an appeal while the fellow has escaped.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: That's what it amounts to. That is -- as far as counsel was concerned, it was met. As far as Leslie Irvin was concerned, he was not before the Court and they refused to consider the motion for new trial.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Therefore, do I understand you said there's no case. I mean you just made the statement, that if he comes back within the 30 days, the appeal would have been entertained, the motion for new trial.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: I would take that position although there's no case over that fact.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Then therefore, that's supposed to mean whether -- although on his behalf, proper steps were taken within the time, and eventually, did come back. Is that all (Inaudible) by -- by the fact that while the steps were taken, he was out of the jurisdiction. That's really the -- we'll get down to that narrow point in the decision.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: We get down to the fact that he -- his appeal was predicated upon the court's ruling on the motion for new trial. And the Court overruled the motion for new trial for the reason that the man was not before the Court. That's what we get right --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Did the trial court file a memorandum when the -- what did they do? Denied the motion or dismissed the motion?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: They denied the motion.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Denied.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Overruled. And I should use the exact (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Suppose the trial court --

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: -- overruled the motion.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Suppose the trial court had done what this Court did in Eisler, held the case, hadn't ruled on it.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: He did for a few days, Your Honor. He held that's been (Inaudible)

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But suppose he held it without -- suppose there had been no cut off within the statutory period and then Irvin would have come back. Would you be here on your -- on your understanding of Indiana law saying that he had no right of appeal?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: I seriously doubt that we would, Your Honor, although I mentioned that earlier. Irvin and I are happily we're not faced with it.(Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes, I understand.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: But if the Court had -- and just to have it, well, let's say, the Court ruled on -- on (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's submitted in the Heisler case. That's what the Court did in the beginning. Suppose it's this Court and Heisler escaped -- Is this Heisler.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: That is in Eisler.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: When Eisler escaped, this Court then just held the appeal which has been -- about to rely after what we saw. Now, suppose your -- your courts have done that, held it.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: I can --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: There has been a proper -- a proper availing of the local remedy within the time the Court held this and then Irvin returned after the filing.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: I take it --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You would have -- you would -- you would indicate that -- you indicate that you wouldn't then make the point you're now making, is that right?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Certainly not the way I'm making it now. No. I -- my point is that he was absent when the Court purported to act on his motion for new trial.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, may I -- do I understand that on the chronology, Mr. Attorney General, the -- that his actual firing was on what, the 19th of January --

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: The 19th, that's correct.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: -- which was the 30th day, was it not?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: And he was brought back from California on what day?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: I don't know the exact day, Your Honor. It was several weeks later. He --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Several weeks within the couple of months, was it?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: It's something like that, yes. I don't know the exact.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Then if I understand you. Had the trial judge not acted on the 23rd, four days later, but had simply sat on the motion and held it until after -- for eight or nine weeks, the issue we now -- that now confronts us wouldn't be here.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: With -- would at least not have a strong a case as we have. I -- I have no authority for that.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Well, I gather, you're telling me that if that had been in the situation under Indiana law, your Indiana Supreme Court, I would then have passed upon all the -- or rather the trial judge would have passed upon the motion, would he not, on the merits?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: I think that he would have. Although I have no real way of knowing that. There is not an issue in this case and it was -- and there is no authority on that precise factual first situation.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: What -- what you do know is what the Indiana Supreme Court held to be the law of Indiana --

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- under these circumstances.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Yes, I do.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: You're telling us you don't know what they would have held if the law of Indiana came --

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- under the supposititious circumstances.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: I am in --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: That's what they held, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Maybe I should explain myself a little more thoroughly here, if it please the Court, that Indiana has a procedure whereby a man can file a belated motion for new trial. This 30-day period is a -- a statutory period, but the Court in his discretion upon matters addressed to him made grant additional time, a belated motion for new trial. And had the judge held that and waited until the man returned, it's conceivable, he should -- he could have awarded an order saying that he has treated this as a belated motion for new trial and handle it very easily. And I think the inference would have been that if he had purported to act it on -- upon the merits, it would -- that would have been the situation, but we don't have that.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Tell me where -- I think you told us that this rule not to hear such motions when the applicant has escaped. It is a rule of the Supreme Court, is it? I mean, well, how is that recorded?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: It -- it's not a -- it's a -- it's case law, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: It is case law.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Yes. We have the two cases, Sutherland versus State of Indiana. They're cited in my brief at page 11 and Sargent versus State. And that the Sargent versus State was cited in Allen versus Georgia by this Court with approval and it's a well-established principle of law again reiterated in --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: When do you say to the argument that after saying all that, the Supreme Court did go ahead and reviewed the case on the merits?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: I don't think they did, Your Honor. I think Justice Finnegan of the -- Judge Finnegan of the Circuit Court of Appeals makes the most astute observation of that. He --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: What they said was, “We have -- because of finality of the sentence, we have reviewed the evidence to satisfy ourselves that there's no miscarriage of justice in this case.” Then they go on to review the -- the matter as you know point my point.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: I -- even though they said that, I think Justice Finnegan of the Circuit Court of Appeals is correct when he says that what they said in regard to the merits was dictum in the case. I -- I think that is obvious because they did say that he had no standing in court. And that was -- that's -- was finality. Then they went ahead and said at -- as though to -- went outside their feeling or wherever you want to put it. Here's a man that's going to die and I know from my own small part in this, it's no easy thing to address yourself to a case where a human life is at stake and I -- I can understand the reluctance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But would -- wouldn't the Supreme Court's opinion at least indicate this much. If they had filed this, there had been a serious violation to this man's constitutional rights. It's non-constant to their escapee rule, they would have had power to review it. It indicates that much, doesn't it?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: It does, Your Honor and there again, the -- the belated -- the post-conviction remedies that Indiana affords, they -- not only can the trial court entertain a belated motion for a new trial, the Supreme Court can entertain a belated appeal. And they could, I take it, have treated this as though it were a belated appeal or sent it back and ask -- order the trial judge to entertain a belated motion for new trial, but they didn't treat it in that fashion.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that -- so that really, you -- you would agree then that what they in effect did was to say, “After looking at this record ourselves, we do not consider this as a case where we have to reach the question as to whether we should or should not exercise our discretion the way of our own state rule.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that it?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: They had not been asked directly to do that but because I think -- I don't think they would have done it in the ordinary case but because in a capital case, I think they took requirements out to do that. Now, I'm only presupposing what the Court had in mind.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What's your limitation of time for a motion for rehearing, in your -- before the Supreme Court?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Before the Supreme Court, it's the petition for rehearing that I -- in -- before the Court, 20 days.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Are -- are the parties limited to one?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Well, I -- I don't quite understand you, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: One petition for rehearing.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: One petition to rehearing, yes. We --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Suppose -- suppose they make a petition, a second petition for rehearing. And I presume that Indiana as in all the other states, while they don't usually take them. Sometimes they do and they discuss the --

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- the merits of that.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: You see --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And under those circumstances, that's not considered it's final, is it until they have --

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- until they find that on the second petition?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. See, the rule --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: It's that about what they've done here in this case if even though they have a rule, they'd gone ahead, considered the merits as they would have on the second petition to rehearing sometimes?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Not directly.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: We frequent -- we frequently bring those up on the ground that although the Court didn't have to act on it, it did.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: No, Your Honor. See, no motion was addressed to them. I -- I say that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But whether the motion is -- motion -- some kind of motion to get them to consider the merits of this case and they did it.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Well, they -- they recite -- they reviewed them, Your Honor but I --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, then that's --

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: I feel --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) consideration isn't it? You -- you can't get away from the fact if they considered the merits and the only one. On the merits, a man was not entitled to get out, not entitled to (Inaudible). Isn't that what they held?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: They said that, Your Honor, yes. But -- but --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And they -- you don't deny they had the power to do that if they wanted to, do you?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: No, I don't deny that they would have had the power --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Acting within their power, they considered this on the merits and said the merits do no justify granting (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: I -- I think this, Your Honor. I say they had the power. I think with proper exercises of the judicial power, that power would have been exercised in conjunction with the motion to that effect if they would have entertained the motion approximate to that effect but they didn't. That (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: They did, afterward, the motion, did they or if they had the case before them and considered it as what they did. Did they go beyond their power in doing what they did, because there was no motion?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Well I again, track back to Judge Finnegan's opinion. I think he's correct in saying they --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I'm not talking about dictum but I'm talking about what they did and what they passed on and what they considered.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Well, I think --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: The other is a label.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: I think what they did passed on and considered, Your Honor, was the fact that he was an escapee at the time he filed his motion for new trial.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You don't think they considered the other at all.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: They didn't discuss it at any length or -- or any --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: They just -- they make up a statement, a -- a cover all statement concerning the merits. They don't take them one at a time and say, this is no indication that they actually examined all of the assignments.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: They said they had looked at them and found there was no good, isn't it?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: They said that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they considered a little more to that. They said that this being a final -- final judgment as they call it, capital case. They'd scrutinized the record and that they reached the conclusion that there was no substance for the constitution question.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: That's what they said.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So if you take it at face value, that he did a conscientious job on what the record satisfy themselves before they hear it to their state ground, state rule that there was not a violation of constitutional right.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: They did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you have to go that far, surely.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: If I might, I would like to address my remaining time to the -- what little there is to the question of the merits of this case arrived here. I take it that -- not that this first question isn't of the merits actually but that -- to the question of the procedures in the trial court. Now, the first question that is raised in that respect is a question on the voir dire examination. Mr. Lockyear read a -- a few of the excerpts that they have incorporated in their brief. Unfortunately, this record as you know, is not printed and we have their excerpts. We have placed a few more excerpts in our brief starting at page 15 and continue on to page 22. And those are only exemplary to this -- the -- the bulk of this big record, this voir dire examination. And to go into all this would have been quite voluminous as far as the briefs are concerned. But with what we have pointed out in our brief and that the citation is therein contained, we respectfully submit to the Court that the portions questioned by the petitioner here are small portions of the voir dire examination occurring early in the examination of these particular witnesses that there are, is much more in the record, contained on the subject that the -- the prospective jurors were examined at length that they demonstrated confusion as to the state of law and that they did qualify under the Indiana statute on that subject.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Are you -- are you -- I'd like to infer from what you've just said that what it set forth in the petitioner's brief about Juror (Inaudible) is not a representative portion of the examination of him?

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: It is not the complete story, Your Honor. And for -- I think there again, we have no record to refer to. But I would refer the Court to pages in the record that that was continued on in transcript volume 5 page 3659, this citation appears on our brief.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: 36 what?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Then the originals here before us.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: 36 what?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Line 19 to page 3667, line 5. That citation, Your Honor, is set out in our brief at -- on page 16 in the middle of the page. And a summation of what is contained therein appears just ahead of that in our brief. And we take the position that the record clearly discloses there that Juror Hensley was qualified under the statute in Indiana. We also take the position, Your Honor, that this is --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It expressed that -- expressed that under the statute of Indiana.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is that anything -- it is (Inaudible) are we special about Indiana statute as qualifications of jurors.

turns.name: RICHARD M. GIVAN
turns.text: I think not, Your Honor. I think it's something that's absolutely essential to the qualification of jurors. We -- in the case of Reynolds against United States, that's referred to in -- by Judge Parkinson, has the District Court opinion on this case. That's in 98 United States 155, United States Report. The principle was laid down -- was stated that in this year or -- and this was in 1879 that they -- and certainly if it prevailed and it prevails today, they said there that in this era of fast communication and public information and so forth that people must, by necessity, be informed of the happenings in their community. And that goes and states why jurors must be qualified although they may entertain some opinion as to what transpired in this particular instant that they nevertheless must be qualified or -- and must -- State must be permitted to qualify them to try a case. Now --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: We'll recess now. Mr. --
